title: SEGMENT 5 — Yamanaka's screen: from twenty-four candidates to four

lines.text: SEGMENT 5 — Yamanaka's screen: from twenty-four candidates to four
Shinya Yamanaka in 2003 is a researcher in his early forties, working at the Nara Institute of Science and Technology in Japan, with a small lab and a big question.
He has been working on embryonic stem cells, and he has noticed that ES cells have a very distinctive genetic signature — they express a particular set of genes that other cell types don't.
Some of these genes encode transcription factors.
Yamanaka makes a bet.

He bets that if he can identify the right transcription factors — the ones that are uniquely active in ES cells and that maintain the pluripotent state — then forcing those same factors into an adult cell might re-impose the pluripotent state.
The factors do the same job in the adult cell that they do in the ES cell.
The adult cell becomes an ES cell.
It is, when you state it baldly, an audacious bet.
It assumes that pluripotency is maintained by a small enough set of factors that you could identify them and recapitulate them.
It assumes those factors are dominant — that adding them to an adult cell can overpower the adult cell's existing program, rather than being neutralized by it.
It assumes the adult cell's chromatin, however locked-down it is, can be reopened by these factors.
None of those assumptions were known to be true.
Most of them, frankly, the field would have bet against.
But Yamanaka had a strategy.
He sat down and made a list of every gene he knew of that was expressed specifically in embryonic stem cells and not in adult tissues.
The list had twenty-four candidates.
Twenty-four genes that the literature, and his own work, had implicated in the pluripotent state.
Some were transcription factors.
Some were chromatin remodelers.
Some had unknown functions.
He didn't know which subset, if any, would do the reprogramming.
So he decided to test them all.
Here is the experimental design, which is gorgeous in its simplicity.
Yamanaka took mouse embryonic fibroblasts — adult cells from a mouse — and he engineered them so that there was a reporter gene that would activate only if the cell entered the pluripotent state.
Specifically, he used a gene called Fbx15, which is on in ES cells and off in fibroblasts.
He linked Fbx15 to an antibiotic-resistance marker.
So: if the fibroblast somehow became pluripotent and activated Fbx15, it would also activate the antibiotic-resistance gene, and the cell would survive antibiotic treatment.
If the cell stayed fibroblast, it would die.
The selection was built in.
You could throw millions of cells at the assay and have only the rare reprogrammed survivors come through.

Then Yamanaka took his twenty-four candidate genes and put all of them, simultaneously, into the fibroblasts using a retrovirus — a viral vector that integrates whatever DNA you put into it into the host genome and forces the cell to express it.
Twenty-four genes, all forced on at once, in a fibroblast that has a reporter for pluripotency.
The result of that first experiment, in 2005, was electrifying.
Some of the fibroblasts — a small fraction — survived the antibiotic selection.
They had activated the Fbx15 reporter.
They had, by the criterion he was using, become pluripotent.
The twenty-four-factor cocktail worked.
But twenty-four factors is way too many.
It's a sledgehammer.
Yamanaka needed to know which ones, specifically, were doing the work.
So he did the obvious next experiment: drop out one factor at a time.
Make a cocktail of twenty-three factors, missing factor number one.
Then twenty-three missing factor number two.
And so on.
For each subtraction, check whether the cells still get reprogrammed.
If a factor is essential, taking it out destroys reprogramming.
If a factor is dispensable, taking it out leaves reprogramming intact.
By systematically subtracting factors, Yamanaka could identify which ones were doing the actual work.
He narrowed it from twenty-four to ten.
Then from ten to four.
The four were Oct4 (also called Pou5f1), Sox2, Klf4, and c-Myc.
We now call this combination OSKM — Oct, Sox, Klf, Myc — pronounced "ozzkim," like a name.
These four factors, together, were sufficient to reprogram an adult fibroblast back into something that looked, by every test Yamanaka could throw at it, like an embryonic stem cell.
Now, what do these four factors actually do?
Let me give you a brief biological tour, because the identities of OSKM are not random — they each have a job.
Oct4 is the master regulator of pluripotency.
It's a transcription factor that, in early embryos, is required to maintain the inner cell mass — the population of cells in the early blastocyst that will become the whole organism.
Without Oct4, embryos arrest at a very early stage.

Within an ES cell or an iPSC, Oct4 sits on top of a giant network of pluripotency genes, keeping them all turned on, while simultaneously suppressing genes that would push the cell toward any specific lineage.
It is, more than any single other factor, the pluripotency gene.
Yamanaka quickly figured out that Oct4 was indispensable — you can swap out the other three, sometimes, but Oct4 has to be there.
Sox2 is Oct4's partner.
The two physically bind to DNA together at many pluripotency genes — they're co-regulators, and they work as a heterodimer.
Sox2 stabilizes Oct4 on its target sites, broadens the set of genes the two of them turn on, and reinforces the pluripotency program.
Like Oct4, Sox2 is essential — you can't reprogram without it.
Klf4 is a transcription factor that does something a little different.

It supports cell proliferation, it helps maintain a particular epithelial-like state that reprogrammed cells need to pass through, and it contributes to the chromatin opening that lets Oct4 and Sox2 access genes they couldn't reach in the original fibroblast state.
Klf4 is somewhat replaceable — closely related family members can sub in — but functionally something Klf-like is needed.
c-Myc is the cell-cycle driver.
We talked about Myc back in the cancer lecture — it's a transcription factor that pushes cells to grow and divide, and it's one of the most frequently mutated oncogenes in human cancers.
Why is Myc here?
Because reprogramming requires the cell to divide many times — chromatin marks get reset during division, and the cell needs to be cycling rapidly for the OSK factors to do their work.
Myc revs up the cell cycle.
It also helps open up chromatin in a more general way, making the genome more accessible to the other factors.
Myc is the accelerator pedal.
Without it, reprogramming still happens, but much more slowly.
With it, things go fast — and, as we'll see, with some risk.
So that's the cast.
Four transcription factors, each doing a specific job, together able to take a skin cell and reset it to pluripotency.
Yamanaka submitted his paper to Cell in 2006, the editors accepted it, and in August of that year, the world of stem cell biology had to be substantially rewritten.
Let's spend the next segment on what the actual paper showed.
